steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.mori: 'mori' is an infinitive, not a finite verb

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the principal parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the principal parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are the principal parts of 'ire' (to go)

hints.to delay: 'to delay' is 'morari' — 'mori' comes from the deponent 'morior'

steps.target: It is a deponent verb — passive in form, active in meaning
choices: It is a deponent verb — passive in form, active in meaning
It is in the passive voice

hints.It is a defective verb: Defective verbs lack certain forms — 'morior' has all forms but is deponent
hints.It is in the passive voice: 'mori' looks passive but is deponent — the meaning is active ('to die')
hints.It is a gerund: A gerund would be 'moriendo' — 'mori' is the present infinitive of the deponent verb

hints.moror, morari, moratus sum: These are the principal parts of 'morari' (to delay) — another deponent
hints.mordeo, mordere, momordi, morsum: These are the principal parts of 'mordere' (to bite)
hints.maneo, manere, mansi, mansum: These are the principal parts of 'manere' (to remain)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would also be 'patria', but 'pro' governs the ablative
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'patriam' — 'pro' + ablative is the construction here
hints.Vocative: Vocative would be 'patria' too, but 'pro' requires the ablative case

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'patria' with -a ending is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension has varied endings — 'patria' is a typical 1st declension -a noun
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'patria' is 1st declension

translation: It is sweet and fitting to die for one's fatherland
